era_context: Middle Bronze Age II. The Old Babylonian Empire was in its final phase of decline. The Hittite Old Kingdom was consolidating power in Anatolia. Egypt was fragmented in the Second Intermediate Period, with the Hyksos gradually gaining influence in the Delta. The Indus Valley civilization was in its final urban or early post-urban phase. Mycenaean Greece was in the Early Bronze Age III, with the foundations of palace culture being laid.

events.title: Continued fragmentation of Mesopotamian political landscape
events.description: The Old Babylonian Empire continued its decline, with regional powers consolidating control. The Kassite kingdom in the Zagros Mountains was growing stronger and would eventually conquer Babylon. The Amorite kingdoms of northern Mesopotamia and Syria were independent. The Sealand Dynasty controlled the southern marshes. This period saw the fragmentation of the unified Mesopotamian state system that Hammurabi had briefly created, returning to a multipolar regional system.

sources.name: Babylonian king lists and administrative texts

events.certainty_note: The fragmentation is attested in cuneiform sources, though specific events in -1702 are not recorded.

events.title: Hittite Old Kingdom consolidation and cultural synthesis
events.description: The Hittite Old Kingdom was synthesizing Anatolian, Mesopotamian, and Indo-European cultural and administrative traditions. Cuneiform writing was adopted for record-keeping. Legal codes were being formulated (later compiled in the Hittite Laws). Religious practices blended Anatolian goddess worship with Indo-European storm god veneration. This cultural synthesis would define Hittite civilization for centuries.

sources.name: Hittite Laws (KBo VI 26) and religious texts, preserved in cuneiform

events.certainty_note: The cultural synthesis is evident in surviving Hittite texts, though the precise dating of individual developments is uncertain.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. However, the broader chronology of the Old Babylonian period remains contested, with multiple competing chronological systems differing by 50–150 years.
historiographic_note: Documentation for -1702 is minimal and indirect. No contemporary records specifically document events of this year. The historical narrative is reconstructed from king lists, administrative texts, and later chronicles. The record is heavily skewed toward Mesopotamia and Anatolia; other regions are essentially invisible in written sources. Archaeological evidence provides some context but cannot pinpoint events to specific years.

graph_edges.note: Mesopotamian fragmentation and Hittite consolidation were concurrent processes reshaping the Near Eastern balance of power.
